tool_output.output:
Based on the provided 10-K filing for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, here are the sections related to the Nippon Steel merger, including business impact, risk factors, and discussion of the merger's effect on operations.

### Source and Page Reference

The extracted information is found in **Part I, Item 1 (Business)** and **Part I, Item 1A (Risk Factors)**. Specific page numbers from the document are included in the citations below.

---

### 1. Section on the Merger and Business Impact (from Part I, Item 1 - Business)

**Page 4** of the filing contains a dedicated section titled **"Agreement and Plan of Merger with Nippon Steel Corporation"** which outlines the business impact:

> On December 18, 2023, the Company entered into...the “Merger Agreement”...Pursuant to the Merger Agreement...Merger Sub will merge with and into the Company, with the Company continuing as the surviving corporation and a wholly owned subsidiary of Purchaser (the “Merger”). On April 12, 2024, the Company obtained the approval of its stockholders required to adopt the Merger Agreement...Subject to the terms and conditions...each share of the Company’s common stock...will, at the Effective Time, automatically be converted into the right to receive $55.00 per share in cash...

> The closing conditions to the Merger include clearance by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (“CFIUS”)...Following referral of the transaction by CFIUS to the President of the United States, on January 3, 2025, the President of the United States issued an order prohibiting the Merger and requiring the parties to abandon the Merger Agreement within thirty days. On January 10, 2025, CFIUS granted an extension of that deadline to June 18, 2025.

> On January 6, 2025, the Company, NSC and Purchaser jointly filed a lawsuit in the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit alleging that the President’s order blocking the Merger was issued for purely political reasons...challenging the decision...On the same day, the Company, NSC and Purchaser also filed a lawsuit in the United States District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania against Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (“Cliffs”), Cliffs’ Chief Executive Officer Lourenco Goncalves, and the President of the...USW...for engaging in a coordinated series of anticompetitive and racketeering activities illegally designed to prevent any party other than Cliffs from acquiring the Company...

---

### 2. Section on Risk Factors Related to the Merger (from Part I, Item 1A - Risk Factors)

**Pages 23-25** contain a dedicated section titled **"Merger-Related Risk Factors"**. Key excerpts include:

**Risk: Failure to complete the Merger**
> If the Merger is not completed, our ongoing business may be adversely affected as follows: (i) we may experience negative reactions from the financial markets, including negative impacts on the market price of our common stock; (ii) some of management’s attention will have been directed to the Merger instead of being directed to our own operations... (iii) the manner in which customers, suppliers and other third parties perceive us may be negatively impacted... (iv) we may experience negative reactions from employees... (v) we will have expended time and resources that could otherwise have been spent on our business; and (vi) we will not obtain the benefits of NSC’s commitments to the Company, including the commitments to invest at least $2.7 billion of capital and to share technology to protect and grow the Company.

**Risk: Significant expenses**
> We have incurred and expect to continue to incur significant expenses in connection with the pending Merger, including legal and investment banking fees and fees incurred in connection with the Merger-related litigation. We expect these costs to have an adverse effect on our operating results.

**Risk: Business uncertainties and contractual restrictions**
> The Merger Agreement requires us to operate in the ordinary course of business and restricts us...from taking certain specified actions...until the pending Merger occurs or the Merger Agreement terminates. These restrictions may prevent us from pursuing otherwise attractive business opportunities and making other changes to our business before completion of the Merger.

**Risk: Impact on business relationships**
> The announcement and pendency of the Merger may adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations...These uncertainties may impair our ability to attract, retain and motivate key personnel...If key employees depart or if our existing business relationships suffer, our results of operations may be adversely affected.

**Risk: Litigation impacting the Merger**
> In connection with the Merger, we (along with our directors and officers) may face lawsuits, disputes and/or other actions...The ultimate resolution of any such proceedings cannot be predicted, and defending against such claims...could result in substantial costs...delay, and diversion of management’s time and resources...

---

### 3. Discussion of the Merger's Effect on Operations (from Part I, Item 1 and Item 7)

The filing discusses the merger's effect on operations in several places:

**From Part I, Item 1 (Business) - Page 23:**
> The Merger Agreement contains customary limitations on U. S. Steel’s ability to engage in such transactions [strategic and organizational opportunities, including acquisitions, divestitures, or asset sales] without the consent of Purchaser.

**From Part I, Item 1A (Risk Factors) - Page 25:**
> The Merger Agreement requires us to operate in the ordinary course of business and restricts us, without the consent of Purchaser, from taking certain specified actions agreed by the parties to be outside the ordinary course of business until the pending Merger occurs or the Merger Agreement terminates.

**From Part II, Item 7 (Management’s Discussion and Analysis) - Pages 58-59:**
> **Share Repurchases**: We do not expect to utilize the remainder of this authorization as the Merger Agreement contains a customary prohibition on our ability to engage in additional share repurchases without the consent of Purchaser.

> **Capital Structure, Liquidity and Capital Allocation**: The Merger Agreement contains customary limitations on U. S. Steel’s ability to incur additional indebtedness without the consent of Purchaser.

> **Business Update**: The filing notes that the parties have substantially complied with all Second Requests, all non-U.S. regulatory approvals have been received, and the only remaining significant closing condition is the CFIUS clearance (which is currently subject to a legal challenge).

**From Part I, Item 1A (Risk Factors) - Page 24:**
> **Expenses related to the pending Merger are significant and will adversely affect our operating results**...We expect these costs to have an adverse effect on our operating results.
